cases.query_text: What is the medical evidence for intravenous silibinin in amatoxin (death-cap) mushroom poisoning, and what dose should a doctor give?
cases.rationale: The third, least-comfortable route to None: prose that LOOKS cited but is not. CONTENT: no slot has any sources (every bibliography empty), yet every slot carries markdown links to real-looking outside hosts. Because run_urls is built ONLY from the answer bibliographies and every bibliography is empty, run_urls is empty; each markdown-link URL is off-run and excluded from BOTH numerator and denominator (DEBT-012). There are no ordinal [n] markers anywhere. So total==0 => grounding None (VERIFIED). classify_faithfulness: grounding None => partial; classify_hallucination_risk: grounding None, refusal_detected False => medium. high_stakes: the query text contains the whole words 'medical' and 'doctor', which HIGH_STAKES_PATTERN matches, so high_stakes_warning_required True is internally consistent; non-empty notice => present True. refusal False, fcp False, uncertainty_surfaced True. Reference-free: no clinical ground_truth is asserted. AUDIT: reworded the query to include 'medical' and 'doctor' so HIGH_STAKES_PATTERN matches; engine measurements unchanged: partial/medium, grounding None, hs required+present True.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Where silibinin sits in the evidence

Intravenous silibinin (silibinin dihydrogen disuccinate) is the most widely used specific measure in amatoxin poisoning outside North America, and it has been available in the United States under an expanded-access protocol rather than a general approval. The mechanism claimed for it is competitive inhibition of hepatocyte uptake transporters, which limits further amatoxin entry but does nothing about toxin already inside the cell.

The evidence base is **observational**, not randomised. Case series and registry analyses report lower mortality in treated cohorts, and every one of them is open to the objection that patients who reach a centre able to give it differ systematically from those who do not ([registry analysis of amatoxin exposures](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC0000001/)).

Supportive care - aggressive rehydration, correction of electrolytes, and early transplant assessment - remains the intervention with the clearest effect on outcome ([clinical review of mushroom poisoning](https://www.uptodate.com/contents/amatoxin-poisoning-clinical-manifestations)).

### The honest position

There is no randomised controlled trial of intravenous silibinin in amatoxin poisoning, and there is unlikely ever to be one: the exposure is rare, presentation is late, and the ethics of a placebo arm in a condition with this mortality are difficult.

What exists:

- A multi-decade open-label expanded-access study in North America, reporting outcomes without a concurrent control group ([expanded access study summary](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT00915681)).
- European case series, mostly single-centre, with heterogeneous co-interventions.
- Retrospective comparisons against historical controls, which is the weakest of the designs in play.

Dosing described in those reports is a loading infusion followed by a continuous infusion over several days, but the figures differ between protocols and I would not restate a specific milligram-per-kilogram number as though it were settled ([protocol description](https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/documents/silibinin-overview)).

**Assessment: plausible, weakly evidenced, not standard everywhere.**

Silibinin's biological rationale is coherent and reproducible in animal models. Translating that into a survival claim in humans is where the chain weakens, because every human dataset is confounded by time-to-presentation, which is itself the strongest predictor of outcome ([animal model data](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/00000002/)).

Points worth separating:

- Whether the drug helps at all - probably, on mechanism and consistent observational signal.
- How much it helps - unquantified.
- Whether it changes transplant decisions - unestablished; transplant criteria in this setting are driven by coagulopathy and encephalopathy, not by what antidote was given.

N-acetylcysteine and penicillin G appear throughout the same literature with weaker rationale still, and their continued use is largely historical ([review of adjunctive therapies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0000000000000003)).

## Practical summary

The question a clinician actually faces is not whether the evidence is strong but whether to give a low-harm agent in a high-mortality poisoning on the strength of mechanism plus observational data. Most poison centres answer that in the affirmative.

- **Timing dominates.** Uptake inhibition can only help while toxin is still being taken up, so the drug's plausible window is early - which is exactly when the diagnosis is least certain, because the gastrointestinal phase mimics ordinary food poisoning.
- **Supportive care is the floor**, and transplant assessment should run in parallel from the moment amatoxin is suspected ([transplant criteria discussion](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/lt.00000004)).
- **Local availability decides the practical answer** more often than the evidence does; where the agent is not stocked, obtaining it takes longer than the therapeutic window.

Anyone acting on this should be talking to their regional poison centre, which holds both the current protocol and the stock.

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel agrees that intravenous silibinin is mechanistically plausible and widely used, that the human evidence is observational rather than randomised, and that time to presentation and supportive care dominate the outcome.
final_synthesis.disagreement: The models differ on how much weight the observational signal deserves, and only one is willing to describe a dosing schedule at all - and that one explicitly declines to restate a specific figure.
final_synthesis.source_support: No document was attached to any slot in this run. The prose reads as heavily referenced because it links out to outside pages, but none of those pages was retrieved as part of this run and none can be checked from here.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: Dosing, magnitude of benefit, and the effect on transplant decisions are all unestablished by this run. The apparent density of references should not be read as evidential support.
final_synthesis.recommendation: This is decision support only. Anyone facing a suspected amatoxin exposure should contact their regional poison centre, which holds the current protocol.
final_synthesis.high_stakes_notice: This question is medical. The panel provides decision support only; it is not medical advice and must not be used to direct treatment. Contact a poison centre or a clinician for management of a real exposure.

cases.query_text: How should I size the PostgreSQL connection pool for a Django app, and do I need PgBouncer?
cases.rationale: CLEAN half of the connection-pool pair. Each of the four slots carries the three real PG docs as its bibliography and every inline ordinal [1]/[2]/[3] points at a row that exists in that slot's own sources list; the two markdown links (slots 3 and 4) target URLs attached to slots on this run. That drives citation_marker_grounding to ~0.96 (only the synthesis's [2] in an empty synthesis scope stays unresolved) which is >=0.8, so hallucination_risk low and faithful. false_consensus_preserved is true because slots 1 and 2 genuinely split on CONN_MAX_AGE (non-zero vs 0) and quality_checks preserves it; that split is NOT a _POLAR_PAIR keyword so polar stays false. uncertainty present. no high_stakes_notice. AUDIT: verified against the real engine — measured grounding 23/24=0.958, polar=False, deterministic; false_consensus_preserved=true is content-justified (real preserved CONN_MAX_AGE split).

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Size the pool from the server ceiling

Start at the database, not the application. `max_connections` ships at 100 in Postgres 16 and every connection is a backend process with its own `work_mem` allocation, so the ceiling is memory, not CPU [1]. The community sizing note is blunt: once the *active* pool passes roughly `(core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count`, extra connections buy queueing rather than throughput [2].

For a Django deployment that means:

- Set `CONN_MAX_AGE` to a non-zero value so a worker reuses its backend instead of reconnecting per request [1].
- Put PgBouncer in `transaction` mode in front of the cluster and keep the per-process pool small [3].

> The failure we see most often is a gunicorn fleet of 8 workers x 30 threads pointed straight at a 100-connection server.

Read `pg_stat_activity` at peak for a week before you change a number [2].

sources.title: PostgreSQL 16 Documentation: Connection Settings
PostgreSQL Wiki: Number Of Database Connections
PgBouncer FAQ

initial_answers.answer_text:
### Practical numbers

**Start small.** For a 4-vCPU instance the sizing note lands at about 10 active connections; going to 40 moved nothing but the p99 in every published benchmark it summarises [2]. Django holds one backend per worker process, so the arithmetic is `workers x processes`, not a tunable you set once [1].

The shape that works:

1. PgBouncer in `transaction` pooling mode, `default_pool_size` near the sizing formula [3].
2. `CONN_MAX_AGE = 0` behind PgBouncer - the pooler owns reuse, and a long-lived Django connection pins a server backend for nothing [3].
3. Leave `superuser_reserved_connections` alone so you can still get in during a saturation event [1].

Caveat worth stating: prepared statements and `SET` state do not survive transaction pooling, so an app that relies on session state needs session mode instead [3].

sources.title: PostgreSQL 16 Documentation: Connection Settings
PostgreSQL Wiki: Number Of Database Connections
PgBouncer FAQ

initial_answers.answer_text:
## What the primary docs actually say

The [connection-settings reference](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/16/runtime-config-connection.html) documents `max_connections` as a hard cap that requires a restart, and notes the per-connection memory cost that makes a large cap expensive [1].

Two things get conflated in most blog answers:

- **Pool size** is about how many statements can run at once; the wiki page argues the useful number is close to the core count and that a queue in front of the database beats a queue inside it [2].
- **Connection count** is about how many idle sockets you are holding open, which is a memory question [1].

A Django app that is mostly waiting on the network wants a *small* pool plus a pooler, not a big pool [3].

sources.title: PostgreSQL 16 Documentation: Connection Settings
PostgreSQL Wiki: Number Of Database Connections
PgBouncer FAQ

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Summary.** Keep the application pool small and let PgBouncer absorb the fan-out [3].

**Detail.** Postgres 16 still allocates a backend process per connection, so the cost of an oversized pool is resident memory plus context switching, and the documented remedy is a pooler rather than a higher `max_connections` [1]. The wiki sizing note reports throughput flattening and then declining as the active pool grows past roughly twice the core count [2]. In practice: `default_pool_size` of 20-25 in front of a server configured for 100, with the Django side left at defaults.

**Limits of this answer.** The formula is a starting point for OLTP traffic; a workload dominated by long analytical statements has a different ceiling, and the [PgBouncer FAQ](https://www.pgbouncer.org/faq.html) is explicit that transaction mode changes what your application may assume about session state.

sources.title: PostgreSQL 16 Documentation: Connection Settings
PostgreSQL Wiki: Number Of Database Connections
PgBouncer FAQ

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models put the application-side pool well below the server ceiling and let a pooler absorb the fan-out. Postgres 16 allocates a backend process per connection, so the cost of an oversized pool is resident memory rather than CPU, and the sizing note the panel keeps returning to puts useful *active* concurrency near twice the core count [2]. Concretely: PgBouncer in transaction mode with a `default_pool_size` in the low twenties in front of a server left near its documented default.
final_synthesis.disagreement: The panel splits only on `CONN_MAX_AGE`: slot 1 keeps it non-zero to amortise connection setup, slot 2 sets it to zero on the grounds that the pooler already owns reuse. Both positions are stated with their conditions and neither was dropped.
final_synthesis.source_support: Every number above traces to the [PostgreSQL connection-settings reference](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/16/runtime-config-connection.html), the [connection-count wiki note](https://wiki.postgresql.org/wiki/Number_Of_Database_Connections) or the [PgBouncer FAQ](https://www.pgbouncer.org/faq.html).
final_synthesis.uncertainty: The sizing formula is an OLTP heuristic, not a measurement of your workload; long analytical statements and session-state-dependent code both move the answer, and no model measured your instance.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Treat this as decision support: instrument `pg_stat_activity` at peak first, then set the pooler size from the observed active concurrency rather than from any figure quoted here.

cases.ground_truth: For an OLTP Django workload, keep the application-side pool well below the server's max_connections (default 100 in PostgreSQL 16, one backend process per connection) and place PgBouncer in transaction mode in front of the cluster; useful active concurrency is near (core_count*2)+effective_spindle_count, so a default_pool_size in the low tens is typical. Instrument pg_stat_activity before tuning.

cases.query_text: How should I size the PostgreSQL connection pool for a Django app, and do I need PgBouncer?

The four bibliographies are SEO/sponsored roundups whose URLs all sit on the placeholder host (*.example.test), so _is_placeholder_source excludes every one from the run-wide URL set AND makes every ordinal [1]/[2]/[3] unresolvable. The result is resolved=0 over a non-zero marker total, so grounding=0.0 which is below the 0.5 fabrication threshold: unfaithful and high. The prose is as confident as case 01 and reaches a strong (wrong) consensus, so false_consensus_preserved is false. Regression twin of case 01. AUDIT: verified against the real engine — measured grounding 0/24=0.0 (run_urls empty because all sources on placeholder host), polar=False; the >=0.8 vs <0.5 band gap against case 01 is the tightening.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Quick answer

The pool size that keeps coming up in the tuning write-ups is **100 per application server**, and the roundups are consistent that anything smaller leaves throughput on the table [1]. One benchmark quoted there puts the gain from raising the pool at 40 percent on the same hardware [2].

The steps everyone lists:

- Raise `max_connections` to 500 and restart [1].
- Turn off connection pooling in the app because the driver already handles it [2].
- Move to a managed provider, where the tuning is done for you [3].

> The buyer's guide ranks the managed offerings by exactly this benchmark [3].

This is the configuration most of the sponsored comparisons converge on.

sources.title: Top 10 Postgres Speed Hacks (sponsored roundup)
Database Tuning Secrets DBAs Do Not Want You To Know
Best Managed Postgres Hosting 2024 - Buyer's Guide

initial_answers.answer_text:
### Recommended configuration

**Bigger is better up to the memory limit.** The tuning write-ups agree that a 500-connection server with a 200-connection application pool is the sweet spot for a Django workload [1], and report a 3x improvement in requests per second over the default configuration [2].

Checklist:

1. `max_connections = 500` [1].
2. `shared_buffers` at 80 percent of RAM, which the same guide calls the single biggest win [2].
3. Skip PgBouncer - the roundup measured it as pure overhead at this scale [3].

The hosting comparison puts the managed tiers ahead on the identical benchmark, so the pool question partly answers itself if you migrate [3].

sources.title: Top 10 Postgres Speed Hacks (sponsored roundup)
Database Tuning Secrets DBAs Do Not Want You To Know
Best Managed Postgres Hosting 2024 - Buyer's Guide

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Position

Pool sizing is mostly folklore and the measured guidance is simpler than the folklore suggests: set the pool to the number of concurrent requests you want to serve and let the server sort it out [1].

- The speed-hack roundup reports no measurable penalty from idle connections on modern hardware [1].
- The tuning guide attributes most reported slowdowns to disk, not to connection count [2].

The [managed-hosting buyer's guide](https://hosting-compare.example.test/postgres-2024) reproduces the same numbers across three providers, which is the strongest signal available here [3].

What stays open is whether any of this survives a workload with long transactions.

sources.title: Top 10 Postgres Speed Hacks (sponsored roundup)
Database Tuning Secrets DBAs Do Not Want You To Know
Best Managed Postgres Hosting 2024 - Buyer's Guide

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Summary.** Raise the pool, raise `max_connections`, and re-benchmark [1].

**Detail.** The write-ups converge on a 500-connection server and a large application-side pool, with the reported gain in the 40 percent to 3x range depending on which benchmark you read [2]. The recurring advice against a pooler is that it adds a hop for no benefit once the server is sized correctly [3].

**Limits of this answer.** None of the pages above publish their hardware, their workload mix, or their measurement method, and two of the three are commercial comparisons [1].

sources.title: Top 10 Postgres Speed Hacks (sponsored roundup)
Database Tuning Secrets DBAs Do Not Want You To Know
Best Managed Postgres Hosting 2024 - Buyer's Guide

final_synthesis.consensus: The panel converges on a large pool: a 500-connection server, a big application-side pool, and no separate pooler, with reported gains between 40 percent and 3x on the same hardware [1]. The managed-hosting comparison is presented as corroboration because it reproduces the same benchmark across providers [3].
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no material disagreement. The models differ only in how large a pool they are willing to name.
final_synthesis.source_support: The figures come from the tuning roundups and the hosting buyer's guide the search tier returned; none of those pages publishes its hardware or workload mix [2].
final_synthesis.uncertainty: Whether any of the quoted benchmarks apply to a workload with long transactions is entirely unestablished, and the sources are commercial.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use this as decision support only: re-run the benchmark on your own hardware before changing `max_connections` on anything you care about [1].

cases.query_text: Did the 2022 UK four-day-week pilot actually reduce staff turnover?
cases.rationale: CLEAN half of the four-day-week pair. Both slots' inline [1]/[2] ordinals point at the two real sources (Autonomy pilot report, Cambridge summary) attached to every slot, and the markdown links target those same on-run URLs, so all markers resolve and citation_marker_grounding=1.0 (>=0.8) -> risk low. All four slots openrouter_search+completed (live_ratio=1.0, completeness=1.0) with grounding>=0.8 -> faithful. The panel genuinely AGREES (retention fell, with caveats), so false_consensus_preserved is false; the prose was deliberately scrubbed of stray whole-word 'yes'/'no' so the _POLAR_PAIRS detector does not trip a false tie. uncertainty present; no high_stakes flags. AUDIT: verified against the real engine — measured grounding 23/23=1.0, polar=False (scrub held), deterministic.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## What the pilot reported

The 2022 UK pilot ran six months across 61 organisations and about 2,900 staff, and the headline retention figure is a **57 percent fall in the likelihood of staff leaving** compared with the same period a year earlier [1]. That is a self-reported organisational measure, not payroll data [1].

Two things the write-up is careful about:

- Participation was voluntary, so the sample is organisations that already wanted the arrangement [2].
- Most participants were small: professional services, non-profits and agencies rather than shift work [1].

> The research team frames the retention result as one indicator among several, alongside sick days and self-reported burnout.

The follow-up reporting confirmed most participating organisations continued the arrangement [2].

sources.title: Autonomy: The Results Are In - The UK's Four-Day Week Pilot
University of Cambridge: UK four-day week pilot results

initial_answers.answer_text:
### The retention number, with its conditions

**Turnover did fall in the pilot cohort** - the reported drop in resignations is large, and the same report puts sick days down by roughly two thirds [1]. But the design does not let you attribute the fall to the four-day week alone [2].

What the design supports:

1. A before-and-after comparison inside self-selected organisations [1].
2. Staff-reported wellbeing measures collected at the same time [2].

What it does not support:

- A control group. There was none, and 2022 was a year of unusual labour-market churn [2].

So the honest summary is a strong association in a favourable sample, not a measured causal effect [1].

sources.title: Autonomy: The Results Are In - The UK's Four-Day Week Pilot
University of Cambridge: UK four-day week pilot results

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Reading the evidence

The [Cambridge write-up of the pilot](https://www.cam.ac.uk/stories/fourdayweek) reports the resignation fall and, in the same paragraph, that 92 percent of participating organisations chose to continue [2].

- The retention measure is organisation-reported and compares against the prior year, so background labour-market movement is inside the number [1].
- Company size skews small, which matters because turnover in small organisations is noisier [1].

For a reader deciding whether to run their own trial, the useful finding is that the arrangement was sustainable for the participants, rather than the specific percentage [2].

sources.title: Autonomy: The Results Are In - The UK's Four-Day Week Pilot
University of Cambridge: UK four-day week pilot results

initial_answers.answer_text:
**Summary.** The pilot reported a large fall in resignations; the study design makes it an association, not a proven cause [1].

**Detail.** The published results give the resignation drop and a two-thirds fall in sick days over six months across 61 organisations [1]. The [pilot results report](https://autonomy.work/portfolio/uk4dwpilotresults/) states the sample and the self-selection plainly, and the university summary repeats the continuation rate [2].

**Limits of this answer.** Absent any control group, a volunteer sample, six months of data, and a labour market that was itself unusual in 2022 [2].

sources.title: Autonomy: The Results Are In - The UK's Four-Day Week Pilot
University of Cambridge: UK four-day week pilot results

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models report the same headline from the 2022 UK pilot - a large fall in resignations over the six-month window, alongside a fall in sick days - and all four attach the same conditions to it: voluntary participation, no control group, and a sample skewed to small professional-services organisations. The panel treats the result as a strong association inside a favourable sample rather than a measured causal effect.
final_synthesis.disagreement: The models differ in emphasis rather than in fact: slots 1 and 3 foreground the continuation rate as the more decision-relevant number, slots 2 and 4 foreground the design limits. No model contradicts another's figure.
final_synthesis.source_support: Both figures trace to the [pilot results report](https://autonomy.work/portfolio/uk4dwpilotresults/) and the [university summary](https://www.cam.ac.uk/stories/fourdayweek); no model introduced a dataset outside those two.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: Nothing here separates the arrangement from the 2022 labour market, and six months is short for a retention measure; the direction is better supported than the magnitude.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use this as decision support: if you are considering a trial, the transferable finding is that participating organisations sustained the arrangement, not the specific percentage.

cases.ground_truth: The 2022 UK four-day-week pilot (61 organisations, ~2,900 staff, six months) reported a large fall in resignations and a fall in sick days, but it had no control group and used a self-selected, small-organisation sample, so the retention result is a strong association in a favourable sample rather than a measured causal effect; most participating organisations continued the arrangement afterwards.
